--- title: "[calibration()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-function}" body-classes: doc-api-page page-navigation: false html-table-processing: none --- ::: {.doc-subject} Assess calibration of predicted survival probabilities at a fixed time. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_metrics.py#L406-L518){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python calibration(surv, predicted, time, *, n_bins=10, conf_level=0.95, format=None) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} Subjects are grouped into `n_bins` bins by their predicted survival probability at `time`. Within each bin the mean prediction is compared against the observed survival, a Kaplan-Meier estimate at `time` for that bin's subjects. A well-calibrated model has the observed values close to the predicted ones (points near the diagonal). ::: ## Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [surv]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Surv]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : The `Surv` response (right-censored or counting-process). [predicted]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [Any]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Predicted survival probability at `time`, one per subject (for example a column of `CoxPH.predict(newdata, type="survival", times=[time])`). [time]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [float]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : The horizon at which predictions are assessed. [n_bins]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [int]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [[10]{.dv}]{.doc-parameter-default} : Number of prediction bins (default 10). Bins are quantile-based. Empty bins are dropped, so ties in `predicted` may yield fewer rows. [conf_level]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [float]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [[0.95]{.fl}]{.doc-parameter-default} : Confidence level for the observed (Kaplan-Meier) interval. [format]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [str | None]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [None]{.doc-parameter-default} : Output format for the returned DataFrame: `None` (default), `"pandas"`, `"polars"`, or `"pyarrow"`. `None` (the default) will auto-detects and prefer Polars if available (falls back to Pandas, then Pyarrow, and raises an error if no DataFrame library is available). ::: ## Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [DataFrame]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : One row per bin with columns `bin`, `n`, `predicted` (mean), `observed`, `observed_lower`, `observed_upper`. Format depends on the `format` parameter. ::: ## Details {.doc-details} Bins are formed by splitting subjects into `n_bins` quantile-based groups of their predicted survival probability. Within each bin the Kaplan–Meier estimate at `time` gives the observed survival, and the mean of the predictions gives the predicted value. A well-calibrated model produces points that lie along the 45-degree diagonal when plotted as observed vs. predicted. Systematic departures from the diagonal indicate over- or under-prediction in that probability range. ## Examples {.doc-examples} Fit a Cox model on the bundled `lung` dataset and assess calibration at one year. Each bin's mean predicted survival is compared against the observed Kaplan–Meier survival for that bin's subjects: ::: {#86e8ebe3 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw lung = gw.load_dataset("lung", backend="polars") y = gw.Surv.right(lung["time"], event=(lung["status"] == 2)) cox = gw.CoxPH().fit(y, lung[["age", "sex"]]) surv = cox.predict(lung[["age", "sex"]], type="survival", times=[365.0], format="pandas") predicted = surv.iloc[0, 1:].to_numpy() gw.calibration(y, predicted, 365.0, n_bins=5, format="polars") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ```{=html}
